name: API Key Leakage via Public GitHub Repositories
fullName: API Key Leakage via Public GitHub Repositories
severity: Critical (especially for cloud or production credentials)
detection: GitHub Secret Scanning Alerts, TruffleHog, Gitleaks, Cloud Provider
scenario: A developer accidentally commits and pushes API keys, cloud credentials or other secrets to a public GitHub repository. These secrets can be harvested by attackers (including bots that monitor GitHub) and used to access critical systems, cloud resources or third-party APIs.
tools: GitHub Advanced Security (secret scanning); TruffleHog, Gitleaks, GitRob; HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault; SIEM (e.g., Sentinel, Splunk) and threat detection systems; Version control auditing (e.g., Git log parsing, commit reviewers)

detSteps.title: Identify the leaked secret type and its access scope
detSteps.detail: Determine exactly what type of credential was exposed: AWS access key (AKIA prefix), GitHub PAT (ghp_), GitLab token (glpat-), Stripe key (sk_live_), or other. The type determines the blast radius — a cloud admin key is far more severe than a read-only API key.

detSteps.title: Check cloud and API provider logs for unauthorised usage of the leaked key
detSteps.detail: Look for API calls using the compromised key from IPs outside your organisation's ranges, especially in the period after the commit was pushed. Most automated bots that harvest GitHub secrets start exploiting keys within minutes of publication.

queries.splunk: index=cloud sourcetype=cloudtrail userIdentity.accessKeyId=[leaked_key_id] | stats count by src_ip, eventName | sort _time
queries.velociraptor: SELECT Laddr, Lport, Raddr, Rport, State, Pid, Name, Ctime FROM netstat() WHERE Rport IN (22,53,80,135,139,443,445,3389,8080,8443) ORDER BY Ctime DESC LIMIT 200
queries.carbon_black: netconn_ipaddr:* AND netconn_port:* | stats count by process_name, netconn_ipaddr, netconn_port, device_name

detSteps.title: Determine the public exposure window
detSteps.detail: Check the git commit history to find exactly when the secret was first committed and when it was removed. Subtract to get the exposure duration. Check if the repo was public during this window — if it was, assume the secret was harvested.

contSteps.title: Immediately revoke the exposed credential at the provider
contSteps.detail: Revoke the key at its source before doing anything else — this is the single most important action. AWS: deactivate in IAM. GitHub: revoke in Developer Settings. Stripe: roll in Dashboard. Do this within minutes of detection.

contSteps.title: Remove the secret from git history — it is not enough to delete the file
contSteps.detail: Deleting the file in a new commit does not remove it from git history — the secret remains accessible in all previous commits. Use git-filter-repo or BFG Repo Cleaner to purge the secret from the full commit history, then force-push.

eradSteps.title: Replace exposed keys with new ones: Generate and distribute new keys securely via vault or secret manager
Audit cloud/API logs: Look for signs of abuse using the leaked key during its exposure period
Validate GitHub repo hygiene: Review commit history and remove any other sensitive information
Block repo or mark private: If it still contains risks or needs re-evaluation

recSteps.title: Restore service access using new secrets: Confirm integrations and pipelines are working with rotated credentials
Re-enable affected users or systems: Once no unauthorised access is detected
Monitor for abuse: Set alerts on any suspicious use of revoked credentials across services
Document impact and confirm clean repo state: Ensure dev teams comply with updated policies

metrics.name: Developer Acknowledgement of Policy
metrics.target: 100% of involved devs within 2 business days
